phrasalVerbs.definition: To be elected for a political job or position.
examples: She got in as a councillor in the local elections.
If the opposition party gets in, policies will change dramatically.
He's hoping to get in as the new union representative.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To have a particular period of time as a holiday or break from work.
examples: I'm getting off early today for a doctor's appointment.
She gets off two weeks for Christmas every year.
We rarely get off before six o'clock.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To stop working, usually at the end of a working day.
examples: We usually knock off around five o'clock.
Let's knock off early today and go for a drink.
The factory workers knock off at the sound of the bell.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To continue doing something in a determined way despite difficulties, opposition, or interruptions.
examples: Despite the setbacks, the team pressed on with the project.
The government decided to press ahead with the controversial reforms.
She pressed ahead despite criticism from her colleagues.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To start doing or working on something in order to achieve an aim.
examples: She set out to prove that the theory was wrong.
They set out to build the most innovative product in the market.
He set out with the intention of changing the industry.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To leave a job or position so that someone else can take over.
examples: The CEO stepped aside to allow for new leadership.
She stepped aside after twenty years in the role.
It's time for the old guard to step aside for younger talent.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To start to employ someone; to accept some work or responsibility.
examples: The company is taking on fifty new graduates this year.
She took on the role of project manager.
We shouldn't take on more work than we can handle.

fillInExercises.sentence: The new mayor ___ after a closely contested election.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get in' means to be elected to a position.

fillInExercises.sentence: Do you ___ off on public holidays?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get off' means to have time off from work.

fillInExercises.sentence: The construction crew ___ at sunset every day.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Knock off' means to stop working for the day.

fillInExercises.sentence: Despite the criticism, she ___ ahead with her plans.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Press ahead' means to continue despite difficulties.

fillInExercises.sentence: She ___ to become the youngest CEO in the company's history.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Set out' means to start working towards a goal.

fillInExercises.sentence: The chairman decided to ___ aside and let someone else lead.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Step aside' means to leave a position for someone else.

fillInExercises.sentence: We're ___ three new interns this summer.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take on' means to employ someone or accept responsibility.

fillInExercises.sentence: Let's ___ off now—we've done enough for today.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Do you think the independent candidate will [get in|get in]?

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: It's possible. The current leader might [step aside|step aside] soon.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: The opposition has [set out|set out] to win back public trust.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: They're [pressing ahead/on (with)|press ahead/on (with)] despite low poll numbers.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Well, I'm going to [knock off|knock off] now and watch the debate.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Lucky you—I don't [get off|get off] until eight tonight.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: I heard the department is [taking on|take on] new staff.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: Yes, we're [setting out|set out] to expand operations significantly.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: That means we'll need to [press ahead/on (with)|press ahead/on (with)] training programmes.

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: The previous manager [stepped aside|step aside] last month, you know.

lines.speaker: Alex
lines.text: I know. What time do you [knock off|knock off] today?

lines.speaker: Fatima
lines.text: I'm hoping to [get off|get off] by six if I finish this report.
